coredata.dc:title: Aluminum-doped carbon quantum dots and carbon nanosheets from electrochemical peeled-off MAX phase and their antioxidant ability
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yueyue
Wang, Yingmin
Li, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Excessive reactive oxygen species levels will cause great harm to the human body, so it’s important to develop a highly effective antioxidant to balance their level. In this study, metal-doped carbon nanomaterials-Al-doped carbon quantum dots (Al-CQDs) and carbon nanosheets (Al-CNSs) were first peeled off from the MAX phase by electrochemical approach. The detailed stripped mechanism was analyzed and demonstrated. The as-prepared Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs exhibited improved antioxidant activity by hydroxyl radicals (·OH) scavenging test, due to the Al doping and thus the improved charge transport ability. As a kind of environment-friendly and non-toxic material, metal-doped carbon nanomaterials could have great application prospects in the field of antioxidants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Al-doping
Carbon quantum dots
Carbon nanosheets
Antioxidant

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principles of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs preparation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process: Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs were prepared under applied electrolytic voltages of 7.5 V and 10 V, respectively; and the schematic representation of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs.
Transmission electron micrographs of electrochemically exfoliated (a) Al-CQDs and (b) Al-CNSs, (c) histogram particle size distribution of Al-CQDs (d) XRD images.
(a) FT-IR spectra, (b) XPS survey spectra, XPS spectra of (c)-(d) C 1 s, (e)-(f) O 1 s, and (g)-(h) Al 2p.
(a) UV absorption spectra and (b) fluorescence spectra of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs, fluorescence spectra under different excitation wavelengths of Al-CQDs (c) and Al-CNSs (d).
(a) PL spectra of control group (H2O), Al-CNSs, Al-CQDs and CNSs at excitation wavelength of 320 nm, (b) CV curves of CQDs, Al-CQDs and control group.
Comparison of free radical scavenging activities of carbon dots antioxidants.

head.ce:title: Aluminum-doped carbon quantum dots and carbon nanosheets from electrochemical peeled-off MAX phase and their antioxidant ability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Material, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Inorganic Nonmetallic Material, School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excessive reactive oxygen species levels will cause great harm to the human body, so it’s important to develop a highly effective antioxidant to balance their level. In this study, metal-doped carbon nanomaterials-Al-doped carbon quantum dots (Al-CQDs) and carbon nanosheets (Al-CNSs) were first peeled off from the MAX phase by electrochemical approach. The detailed stripped mechanism was analyzed and demonstrated. The as-prepared Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs exhibited improved antioxidant activity by hydroxyl radicals (·OH) scavenging test, due to the Al doping and thus the improved charge transport ability. As a kind of environment-friendly and non-toxic material, metal-doped carbon nanomaterials could have great application prospects in the field of antioxidants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are single-electron reduction products of oxygen in the body, including singlet oxygen (1O2), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), superoxide radical (·O2 –), hydroxyl radical (·OH), and nitric oxide (NO) [1]. Many chemical drugs such as anti-cancer agents, antibiotics, insecticides, and anesthetics can induce the production of ROS to prevent or treat various diseases. They can repair or scavenge damaged cells and prevent cell death due to oxidative stress when the body maintains a balanced ROS level. However, excessive ROS may cause a series of problems such as cell damage and apoptosis, aging, cancer, and DNA damage [2,3]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop efficient antioxidants to offset the abnormal elevation of ROS levels.

Carbon nanomaterials including fullerene, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon nanosheets (CNSs), graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), etc. have been widely investigated and exhibited great application prospect in the fields of antioxidants due to their large specific surface area, excellent hydrogen donation and electron transfer ability, biocompatibility and low toxicity [4–7]. Zhang Wendi et al. [8] studied the scavenging activity of CQDs by DPPH· and gave a coupled hydrogen atom transfer mechanism for DPPH· scavenging. Jun-Jie Yin et al. [9] analyzed the free radical-scavenging ability of water-soluble fullerenes by the ESR signal, and proved that fullerene derivatives could reduce the production of intracellular ROS, including DPPH·, 1O2 and ·OH through the donation of a hydrogen atom or electron transfer. However, the application of the above materials is still greatly limited by their poor water solubility, large molecular structure, low radical scavenging efficiency, and complex preparation process [10,11]. As a result, a properly modified method with a simple operation process could be exploited to improve antioxidant activity and other related applied abilities of carbon nanomaterials for their application.

Element doping is an effective approach to adjust or improve the physicochemical properties of carbon nanomaterials. Heteroatoms could modify their electronic structure by affecting the interactions between π and n states, and further regulate their surface electron density or band gap [12]. Up to now, N-doped GQDs, CNTs, CNSs [13–15], B-doped graphene [16], P-doped CNSs [17] and S-doped CQDs [18], etc. were reported in succession. And our previous study has demonstrated that the Cl-doped CQDs and P-doped CQDs could improve the scavenging behavior of ROS [10,19]. Compared to non-metal atoms, metal atoms are easier to lose electrons and have more unoccupied orbitals. Thus, metal atoms can effectively increase the charge density of doped carbon nanomaterials and improve charge transfer between doped carbon nanomaterials and other materials around [10]. According to previous research, the easier the antioxidant loses electrons, the stronger its ability to scavenge free radicals. Therefore, metal atoms doped carbon nanomaterials will display high antioxidant activity. Up to now, although there are many reports on metal atom-doped carbon nanomaterials, such as Ln-doped CQDs (Ln = Yb, Nd) [20], Pd-doped CQD [21], Pt-doped CQD [21], Ru-doped CDs [22], Co-doped CQDs [23,24], Cu-doped CQDs [25], Ce-doped CQDs [26], Ru-doped CNTs [27] and Gd-doped CQDs [28], little work has focused on their antioxidant activity.

In this work, we first prepared aluminum-doped carbon quantum dots (Al-CQDs) and carbon nanosheets (Al-CNSs) from Ti3AlC2 MAX (M, A and X represent transition metal elements, principal elements, and carbon or nitrogen) phase by electrochemical method and investigated their antioxidant activity by hydroxyl radical (·OH) scavenging. Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs exhibited improved scavenge efficiency than the undoped ones. The high antioxidant activity is attributed to the doping of Al and thus the enhanced electron transfer ability.

Ti3AlC2 block (Kaiene Ceramic Powder Company), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, analytical reagent), titanium dioxide (TiO2, analytical reagent), terephthalic acid (C8H6O4, analytical reagent), graphite rods, phosphate buffer saline (PBS), potassium chloride (KCl, analytical reagent), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6], analytical reagent), potassium cyanide (KCN, analytical reagent).

The preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 . The Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs were prepared by potentiostatic electrolysis using a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation. High purity Ti3AlC2 block (∼1.5 cm in diameter and ∼ 2 mm in thickness), platinum wires, and NaOH standard solution (0.1 M) were used as the working, counter electrodes, and electrolyte, respectively. The Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs were prepared under applied voltages of 7.5 V and 10 V, respectively. After electrolysis, the solution was filtered out large Ti3AlC2 particles and insoluble impurities using a 220 nm aqueous filter head and dialyzed for 3 d using a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 8000–14000 Da to remove the residual electrolyte. Finally, aqueous solutions of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs were obtained, respectively.

TiO2 can generate ·OH in the UV light (365 nm) and terephthalic acid (TPA) captures ·OH radicals to form fluorescent product 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (excitation wavelength: 315 nm, emission peak: 430 nm). The ability of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs to scavenge ·OH was represented by detecting fluorescent intensity after the scavenge reaction. First, a 2 mL solution containing 25 mM PBS, 0.5 mM terephthalic acid (TPA), 50 μg/mL TiO2, and 50 μg/mL Al-CQDs or Al-CNSs were prepared. The emission spectra of the solutions were tested after UV illumination (8 W, 365 nm) for 1 h. The final results of the test were subtracted from the fluorescence generated by Al-CQDs or Al-CNSs themselves.

For all antioxidants, the deionized water is used as a standard and the decrease in PL spectra at 430 nm of the control and sample was measured. The RSA toward ·OH was estimated using the following equation:
where Ic and Is refer to the intensity of the control and experimental groups at 430 nm, respectively.

The electrochemical cyclic voltammetry was tested at an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, Shanghai Chenhua) with CQDs (which were obtained by the electrochemical method in our previous study [29]) or Al-CQDs modified a commercial glassy carbon electrode (GCE) as a working electrode, a Pt wire as a counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. The GCE electrode was burnished with alumina powder before the experiment and electrode calibration, and then ultrasonicated for 20 min in ethanol and deionized water, respectively. 50 μL of Al-CQDs (200 μg/mL) solution was then dropped onto the pretreated GCE electrode until dry. Then the CV test started in a solution of potassium chloride (0.1 M) containing 0.5 mM potassium ferricyanide and 0.5 mM potassium cyanide.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2010 electron microscope. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were measured using a DMAX-RB (λ = 0154406 nm) X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded using an ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer with an Al monochromatic Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) source. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded using a NEXUS spectrometer 670. Ultraviolet − visible spectroscopy was performed using a TU-1900 spectrophotometer and photoluminescence spectra were obtained using a 4500 Fluorimeter.

TEM images of the samples showed that the Al-CQDs were similar spherical shapes and had good dispersibility (Fig. 2 a). From the plotted histogram particle size distribution of Al-CQDs (Fig. 2c), the average particle size of 2.7 nm was obtained. As prepared Al-CNSs (Fig. 2b) had a few-layer structure. However, no characteristic peaks were shown in the XRD patterns in Fig. 2c, indicating that the as-prepared Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs were in an amorphous state.

XPS and FT-IR spectra were conducted to analyze the composition, element content, and surface functional groups of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the similar stretching vibrations at 3200–3500 cm−1, 1667 cm−1, and 1260–1410 cm−1 of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs demonstrated that a large number of oxygen-containing groups present on the surface of two samples [30]. What’s more, the appearance of Al-O peaks at 500 ∼ 725 cm−1 demonstrated that a certain amount of Al was present in the samples. In the XPS survey spectra (Fig. 3b), the peaks at 284.5 eV, 532.1 eV, and 72.9 eV correspond to the C 1 s, O 1 s, and Al 2p orbitals were shown, respectively, which further displayed CQDs and CNSs were composed of carbon, oxygen, and aluminum. Calculated by the XPS data, the content of Al in Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs was 1.33% and 2.15%, respectively. The narrow XPS spectrum of C 1 s of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs (Fig. 3c and d) both showed C = C/C–C, C = O, O-C = O, and C-O/C-O-Al bonds, indicating the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of two samples. The narrow XPS spectrum of O 1 s of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs (Fig. 3e and f) further shows C = O, H2O, C-OH/C-O-C, Al-O-Al bonds, indicating that Al exists in CQDs and CNSs. Moreover, except for the Al-O bond, the Al-C bond was also found in the narrow Al 2p spectrum (Fig. 3g and h), demonstrating that part of Al bonded with C.

Due to the quantum confinement effect of their small size, Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs exhibited similar UV absorption performance in Fig. 4 a. The characteristic absorption peaks at 200–250 nm and 280 nm could be attributed to n-π* transitions between the oxygen-containing group and the sp2 carbon and π–π* transition of the electrons in the sp2 carbon conjugate structure, respectively [31,32]. When the Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs were excited at 320 nm, they both exhibited strong fluorescence in the range of 450–460 nm (Fig. 4c, d). Under the excitation of 320 nm, they exhibited fluorescence emission at about 450 nm (Fig. 4b). The UV absorption and fluorescence emission phenomenon further indicated small-size Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs formed after electrochemical stripping Ti3AlC2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principles of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation process and schematic representation of Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs are shown in Fig. 1. Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs were prepared in a two-electrode system, respectively. The process of electrolysis of the Ti3AlC2 phase includes two stages, the details are as follows:

First, there is always water electrolysis in the whole electrolysis process. In this process, H+, hydroxyl (·OH) and oxygen radicals (·O), H2 and O2 will successively produce on the surface of two electrodes [33,34] (Equation 1, 2 and 3). In the Ti3AlC2 phase, it is known that the electronegativity difference of the Ti-Al bond is 0.07 eV, which is a metallic bond. And that of the Ti-C bond is 1.01 eV, corresponding to a polar bond. As a result, the bonding strength of the Ti-C bond is stronger than that of the Ti-Al bond. Thus, the Ti-Al layer was first opened rapidly from the edges and surface area of Ti3AlC2 under the attack of OH–, H+, ·OH and ·O radicals provided by the high concentration of sodium hydroxide and the above reactions. With the oxidized and hydroxylated Ti3AlC2 electrode surface, Na+, OH–, and radicals could further embed into the interlayers between Ti and Al because of the increased layer space. The further rapid reaction between invasion groups and the Al layer, along with the external surface pressure from the aggregation of H2 and O2 bubbles promoted Al dissolution to generate Al(OH)3. It is a white solid in sodium hydroxide solution (Equation 4), which could be observed during the reaction. After the Al layer extraction, Ti3C2 MXene was formed on the surface of the Ti3AlC2 electrode.
(1) 2H2O = 2H2 + O2
(2) H2O - e- = ·OH + H+
(3) H2O - e- = ·O + H+
(4) Al3+ + 3OH– = Al(OH)3↓

Second, in the high-voltage electrolysis environment, high energy provided by high voltage could break the Ti-C bond. After Al removing, Na+, OH–, ·OH and oxygen (·O) radicals enter into the interlayer more easily, and the invasion groups could further combine with fractured Ti to form (Equation 5). The only leaving monolayer of carbon atoms would be peeled off from the surface of the Ti3AlC2 electrode, and the as-prepared CNSs are formed in the solution.
(5) Ti4+ + 4OH- = H4TiO4

However, when the applied voltage is not high enough, with the formation of some ultrafine CNSs, a large number of C-O bonds or C = O bonds were produced between carbon nanolayers since a great quantity of OH–, H+, ·OH and ·O radicals. If CNSs surrounded by the mixed epoxy chain composed of epoxy and carbonyl pair groups, C–C bonds may further break up during the electrolysis oxidization reaction, by which the bridging O atoms in the epoxy lines are removed. A large number of broken C–C bonds lead to the formation of CQDs eventually [35].

For ·OH scavenging, electron transfer between CQDs and ·OH is attributed to the main mechanism [41]. Therefore, the electron transfer ability of Al-CQDs is investigated in this work. According to the redox curves measured by cyclic voltammetry, the peak current intensity of the three electrodes is Al-CQDs > CQDs > bare GCE. This indicated that as-prepared Al-CQDs have better charge transfer ability than CQDs and bare GCE. In terms of the mechanism of antioxidants, the easier the electrons lose, the greater the ·OH scavenging ability obtains. The improved electron transfer ability due to the Al doping enhances the electron transfer from Al-CQDs to ·OH. Thus Al-CQDs have higher scavenging efficiency than CQDs. Besides, Al-CNSs as a kind of two-dimensional material have an ultrahigh specific surface area (193 m2 g−1), which are more conducive to the absorption of ·OH and electron transfer. And correspondingly, Al-CNSs have higher scavenging efficiency than Al-CQDs.

In this work, Al-CQDs and Al-CNSs were successfully prepared by a simple and rapid electrochemical approach from the Ti3AlC2 phase. They exhibited a highly efficient ·OH scavenging activity because of the Al doping, and thus the improved charge transport ability. On account of the higher Al doping content and ultra-high surface area, the Al-CNSs have better ·OH scavenging activity than Al-CQDs. This work sheds new light on the preparation of metal-doped CQDs and CNSs, and provides a promising strategy for the application of Al-CQDs in antioxidants.

Yueyue Zhao: Data curation, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yingmin Wang: Visualization, Investigation, Validation. Yan Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22175019, 52002007), and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities and the Youth Teacher International Exchange & Growth Program (QNXM20220019).
